message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  We determined CR1, CD35 the C3b, C4b receptor density, C3b/C3bi and C4d deposits densities on Erythrocytes (E) in 51 COVID-19 patients undergoing O
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  therapy or assisted ventilation in ICU units in Rheims France. A clear acquired decrease of CR1 density of E from COVID-19 patients was observed, particularly among fatal cases, and paralleling several severity parameters.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>Deposits of C4d largely above values observed in normal individuals, mostly without C3 deposits, have been observed in more than 80% of the patients, reminiscent of the sub endothelial pericapillary deposits in organ transplant rejection, already observed on E in parallel, as well as also observed on E in clinical SLE flares.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>Conversely, significant C3 deposits were only observed among ¼ of the patients. The decrease of CR1/E density, and the detection of virus spike, C3 or C4 fragment on E, among COVID-19 patients, are likely to be two aspects of the same phenomenon of immune complexes or complement fragment coated cell debris handling and clearance.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>Measurement of C4d deposit on E might represent a way for assessing inflammation and complement activation occurring in organ capillaries. CR1/E decrease might represent a cumulative index of complement activation in COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>Taken together, these original findings stress on the participation of the complement regulatory proteins in that disease and evidence that E matter in immune mechanisms in COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>The use of CR1, or CR1-like molecules with the aim of down regulating complement activation and inflammation for therapy should also be considered.</jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>HIGHLIGHTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Acquired decrease of CR1 on E in COVID-19 patients, particularly among fatal cases, and paralleling several severity parameters.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Large C4d deposits on E in most patients, reminiscent of the pericapillary deposits in organ transplant rejection, already observed on E in parallel, as well as on E from SLE flares.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>C4d deposit on E, a possible way for assessing inflammation and complement activation in organ capillaries.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Decreased CR1/E, a possible cumulative index of complement activation in COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The use of CR1 or CR1-like molecules for down regulating complement activation for therapy should also be considered.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Acquired decrease of the C3b /C4b receptor (CR1, CD35) and C4d deposits on Erythrocytes from ICU COVID-19 Patients
